notes: Single-sideband FM technique for two-way broadcast-quality video over twisted pair phone wire. Product configuration: PBX switch (5-100 users) + modem-sized desktop device. Below $3000/user. Appears obsolete; company did not survive.
Existing phone line used as VidModem transport. VidModem key value proposition: no rewiring required. Same infrastructure later used for DSL broadband.
VidModem interfaced with ATM as wide area transport. Telcos promoting ATM backed VidModem. ATM itself declined as IP/MPLS and Ethernet WAN superseded it.
Aberdeen's reference case for telco deployment risk: ISDN applications died waiting for telco buildout. VidModem faced same risk. ISDN largely obsolete by 2010.
IP-based video conferencing standard. Aberdeen does not mention H.323 (ratified 1996) — competitor to VidModem approach. H.323 ultimately displaced analog FM video.
Aberdeen coins 'CTVI' (Computer Telephony Video Integration) for VidModem's seamless computer-phone-video integration. CTI became standard call center technology without VidModem.
Proprietary analog technique enabling NTSC PAL S-VHS broadcast quality video over single twisted pair alongside voice. Novel analog approach in an increasingly digital world.
IP-based browser-native video conferencing that ultimately solved the problem VidModem attempted. Zoom Teams Meet built on WebRTC/SIP foundations.
VidModem switch installed next to PBX. No rewiring required. PBX itself gradually displaced by VoIP/UCaaS platforms.
